title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report and the Grafana dashboard — the linear memory climb pattern is very helpful.

A couple of key things to narrow this down: **What version of TaskFlow are you running, and did this start after a specific upgrade or change?** If you can pinpoint when the daily restarts became necessary (e.g., "started after upgrading from 2.3 to 2.4" or "started after we enabled feature X"), that would significantly help us isolate the cause.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: We're running TaskFlow v2.3. We upgraded from v2.2 about a week ago, and that's exactly when the daily restarts became necessary. Before the upgrade on v2.2, the server was rock solid — we never had memory issues. So yeah, the timing lines up perfectly with the v2.3 upgrade.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for confirming the timing — a regression tied to the v2.3 upgrade is very useful to know.

Next question: **Does the memory growth correlate with active user activity, or does it climb even when the system is idle?** For example, if you restarted the server on a weekend when few users are active, does the memory still climb at the same rate? And have you noticed whether any specific operations make it worse — things like file attachments, real-time updates/websockets, or bulk task imports? Any change in the rate of climb during heavy vs. light usage would help us pinpoint the leaking component.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
Good question. The memory growth definitely correlates with user activity. We actually did have a quieter period over the weekend — only maybe 20-30 users active — and the memory climbed much more slowly. On a normal weekday with all 200 users active, it hits 3-4GB by end of day, but on the weekend it barely reached 1GB before we restarted. So it's clearly tied to usage volume.

As for specific operations — now that you mention it, the v2.3 changelog mentioned 'improved real-time notifications' as one of the new features. I remember noticing that because we were excited about it. I can't say for sure that's the culprit, but the timing matches. We haven't done any bulk imports or anything unusual — it's just normal day-to-day task management, creating and updating tasks, commenting, etc. Every API request seems to contribute to the growth, it's not like one specific action spikes it.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
The evidence is pointing strongly at the new real-time notifications feature introduced in v2.3. My working theory is that **websocket connections or event listeners from the real-time notification system aren't being properly cleaned up** — each user session (or reconnection) accumulates resources that never get released, which explains the linear, usage-correlated memory growth.

To test this: **Can you check the number of active websocket connections on your server right now (or at end of day) versus shortly after a restart?** For example, running `ss -s` or `netstat -an | grep ESTABLISHED | wc -l` on the server would show active connections. If we see, say, thousands of connections late in the day when only 200 users are active, that would confirm connection leaking. Alternatively, if your Grafana dashboard tracks open connections or file descriptors, that would work too.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I just checked — we're about 6 hours into the day and memory is sitting around 2.5GB. I ran `ss -s` and the number of established TCP connections looks pretty normal, around 220-230, which lines up with our ~200 active users. So it's not like we have thousands of stale connections piling up. I also checked file descriptors with `ls /proc/<pid>/fd | wc -l` and that's not wildly high either.

So the connections themselves seem fine, but the memory is definitely climbing steadily. Whatever is leaking, it seems like it's something internal rather than actual websocket connections stacking up. Given that every API request seems to contribute to the growth — our users make maybe 25 requests per hour each on average — I wonder if something is being allocated per-request inside the notification system rather than per-connection. Like maybe each request is registering for notifications somehow and that registration never gets cleaned up? I'm just speculating though, I don't know the internals.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Great detective work ruling out connection leaking — that's very helpful. Your instinct about per-request allocations inside the notification system is a credible theory.

Two things that would help us nail this down:

1. **Can you disable the real-time notifications feature?** Some TaskFlow deployments allow this via a config flag (e.g., `TASKFLOW_REALTIME_ENABLED=false` or similar in your config). If you can turn it off and run for a day, we'd know definitively whether the notification system is the source of the leak — if memory stays flat, we have our answer.

2. **What runtime/platform is your TaskFlow instance running on?** (e.g., Node.js, Java/JVM, Python) — this will help us recommend the right profiling tool so developers can see exactly what objects are accumulating.

Even just the first one would be a huge signal.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I'm not sure if there's a config flag to disable real-time notifications — I haven't seen one in the docs, but I'll dig through the configuration options today and see if I can find something like that. If I can turn it off, I'll run it for a day and report back on whether the memory stays flat. That would be a pretty definitive test.

As for the runtime, TaskFlow runs on Go — so it's a compiled binary. I know Go has built-in profiling tools like pprof, if that helps the developers look into it.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak in v2.3 real-time notification system causes linear memory growth (~500MB → 4GB+/day)","problem":"Self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3 instances experience steady, usage-correlated memory growth from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ over a full workday with 200 active users. Page loads degrade to 10+ seconds and API requests time out, requiring daily restarts. The issue is a regression introduced in the v2.2 → v2.3 upgrade.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The 'improved real-time notifications' feature introduced in v2.3 is allocating per-request resources (likely event listener registrations, notification queue entries, or subscriber records) that are never released. This is not a connection leak — active TCP connections and file descriptors are normal — but rather an internal data structure that grows with every API request. At ~200 users × ~25 requests/user/hour, approximately 5,000 objects/hour accumulate without cleanup, producing the observed linear memory climb.","reproduction_steps":["Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a server with monitoring (Grafana or equivalent)","Start the server and note baseline memory (~500MB)","Simulate normal usage with ~200 concurrent users performing standard task CRUD and commenting","Observe memory growth over 6-8 hours — expect ~300-400MB/hour growth under full load","Verify TCP connection count remains proportional to active users (not accumulating)","Compare with TaskFlow v2.2 under identical load — expect flat memory usage"],"environment":"Self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2), Go runtime, ~200 active users on weekdays. Server details not specified but memory behavior is consistent and reproducible day-over-day per Grafana dashboards.","severity":"high","impact":"All 200 users on the instance experience progressively degrading performance throughout the day. By late afternoon, the application is effectively unusable (10+ second page loads, API timeouts). Requires daily manual restarts as a workaround. Any self-hosted v2.3 deployment with moderate user counts is likely affected.","recommended_fix":"1. Diff the v2.3 real-time notification code against v2.2 and identify per-request allocations — look for event registrations, subscriber maps, notification queues, or goroutine spawns tied to HTTP request handling that lack corresponding cleanup/deregistration. 2. Take a pprof heap profile (`go tool pprof http://host:port/debug/pprof/heap`) early in the day and again after several hours — compare to identify which object types are accumulating. 3. Ensure all per-request notification resources are released when the request completes (e.g., deregister listeners, close channels, remove map entries). 4. Consider whether a config flag like `TASKFLOW_REALTIME_ENABLED=false` should be exposed to allow operators to disable the feature as a mitigation.","proposed_test_case":"Start a TaskFlow v2.3 instance, simulate 200 concurrent users making 25 requests/hour each for 8 hours, and assert that heap allocations (measured via pprof) remain bounded — total memory should not exceed baseline + a reasonable per-connection overhead (e.g., stay under 1GB). Compare against the same test on v2.2 as a regression baseline.","information_gaps":["Exact pprof heap profile data showing which object types are accumulating (developer can gather this)","Whether a TASKFLOW_REALTIME_ENABLED config flag exists to disable the feature for confirmation (reporter is investigating but developer can check source)","Whether the leak is in the notification publisher side, subscriber side, or both"]}
